json.Diagram Text:
DataHub.local is a home laboratory that will enable you to create an complete data platform like [Snowflake](https://www.snowflake.com/) but that could run in low-end devices such as RaspberryPi, or OrangePi at home.

As a home laboratory its main personal benefits are:

- **Hands-On Experience**
- **Experimentation and Innovation**
- **Problem-Solving Skills**
- **Portfolio Development**
- **Self-Directed Learning**
- **Understanding of Hardware and Networking**
- **Passion and Motivation**

## Goals

- Deploy and maintain a general purpose architecture based on Kubernetes.
- Implement the lastest Devops pratices to automate the building, testing, and deployment of software.
- Incorporate monitoring and security tools to track the performance of software and identify potential issues of any kind.
- Desing and implement a scalable system that allows for the efficient collection, storage, processing, and analysis of Data.

json.diagramDescription:
DataHub.local is a home laboratory that will enable you to create an complete data platform like [Snowflake](https://www.snowflake.com/) but that could run in low-end devices such as RaspberryPi, or OrangePi at home.

As a home laboratory its main personal benefits are:

- **Hands-On Experience**
- **Experimentation and Innovation**
- **Problem-Solving Skills**
- **Portfolio Development**
- **Self-Directed Learning**
- **Understanding of Hardware and Networking**
- **Passion and Motivation**

## Goals

- Deploy and maintain a general purpose architecture based on Kubernetes.
- Implement the lastest Devops pratices to automate the building, testing, and deployment of software.
- Incorporate monitoring and security tools to track the performance of software and identify potential issues of any kind.
- Desing and implement a scalable system that allows for the efficient collection, storage, processing, and analysis of Data.

diagramTypes.name: Linear Data Flow
diagramTypes.description: A left-to-right flow showing data moving through a mostly linear stage sequence: source → ingestion → processing → storage → consumption; keep the main path explicit and do not introduce branching unless the topic clearly requires it
key_elements: Source nodes (e.g., Kafka, S3, APIs)
Processing/transform boxes (e.g., Spark, dbt, Flink)
Storage layers (e.g., Snowflake, Delta Lake)
Consumption endpoints (e.g., BI tools, ML models)
Directional arrows with data volume or latency labels
Stage grouping with swimlanes or color bands
diagramTypes.style_hint: Strict left-to-right layout, equal-width stage columns, technology icons inside labeled boxes, thin connector arrows with optional data-type annotations, and a single readable main sequence without collapsing stages or inventing side branches
diagramTypes.color_palette: Deep blue for sources, teal for processing, purple for storage, orange for consumption, white background, light gray connectors

diagramTypes.name: Architecture Blueprint
diagramTypes.description: A top-down or isometric view of cloud components interacting — services, VPCs, network boundaries, and data flows
key_elements: Cloud provider service icons (compute, storage, networking)
VPC / network boundary dashed outlines
Availability zones and regions
Labeled connection arrows (HTTPS, gRPC, SQL, etc.)
Security perimeters (firewalls, WAF, IAM boundaries)
External actors (users, third-party APIs)
diagramTypes.style_hint: Layered zones from external → edge → compute → data, each zone with a distinct background shade, official cloud icons where possible
diagramTypes.color_palette: AWS orange / GCP blue / Azure cyan for services, light gray zone backgrounds, red for security controls, white background

diagramTypes.name: Comparison Matrix / Table
diagramTypes.description: A structured side-by-side comparison of two or more technologies, patterns, or approaches highlighting pros, cons, and ideal use cases
key_elements: Column headers (one per option being compared)
Row attributes (criteria: cost, latency, scalability, complexity, etc.)
Rating indicators (checkmarks, dots, bars, or scores)
Pros/cons sections per column
Highlighted 'best for' row at the bottom
Optional icons per technology
diagramTypes.style_hint: Clean table grid, alternating row shading, column headers with color accent per option, bold verdict row at bottom, and strict row-column alignment so criteria stay horizontal and are not turned into a flow or timeline
diagramTypes.color_palette: Each column has a distinct header color (blue, green, orange), light gray row alternation, white background, dark text

diagramTypes.name: Tech Stack Pyramid
diagramTypes.description: A hierarchical layered diagram showing the levels of a technology stack from infrastructure at the base to applications and interfaces at the top
key_elements: Horizontal tiers stacked bottom to top
Layer labels (Infrastructure, Compute, Storage, Transform, Serve, Consume)
Technology logos or names inside each layer
Arrows or gradients indicating abstraction direction
Optional capability annotations per layer
diagramTypes.style_hint: Trapezoid or triangle pyramid shape, wider at base (infrastructure) narrowing toward top (user-facing tools), each layer in a distinct color band, with technologies kept inside their assigned tier and not rearranged into a process flow
diagramTypes.color_palette: Dark gray at base grading through blue, teal, green, to orange at the top, white text labels, white background

diagramTypes.name: Venn Diagram of Trade-offs
diagramTypes.description: Overlapping circle diagram illustrating competing constraints, trade-offs, or intersecting concepts (e.g., CAP Theorem, Cost/Speed/Quality)
key_elements: 2–4 large overlapping circles
Bold labels inside each circle (one concept per circle)
Intersection zone labels describing the overlap
Central intersection label (the 'sweet spot' or impossibility)
Optional icons or illustrations inside each circle
diagramTypes.style_hint: Large translucent overlapping circles, each in a distinct color, clearly readable intersection labels, centered layout with minimal clutter
diagramTypes.color_palette: Soft transparent fills (blue, red, yellow, green), white or light gray background, dark bold text labels

diagramTypes.name: Continuous Loop / Cycle
diagramTypes.description: A circular diagram illustrating iterative, repeating processes such as CI/CD pipelines, MLOps lifecycles, or feedback loops; show a closed cycle where the last stage reconnects to the first rather than a linear DAG
key_elements: Circular or elliptical arrangement of process stages
Labeled nodes/steps along the circle
Directional arrows indicating flow direction
Central label describing the overall cycle name
Optional icons per stage
Highlighted entry/exit points or key metrics
diagramTypes.style_hint: Circular layout with evenly spaced stage nodes, curved arrows connecting each stage in sequence, a central label in the middle of the loop, and a visibly closed return path so the diagram reads as cyclical instead of top-to-bottom
diagramTypes.color_palette: Gradient color progression around the circle (blue → purple → orange → green), white background, dark text labels

diagramTypes.name: Hub and Spoke
diagramTypes.description: A central component (hub) connected to multiple surrounding systems or consumers (spokes), illustrating a centralized architecture pattern
key_elements: Large central hub node with label and icon
Radially arranged spoke nodes around the hub
Labeled bidirectional or directional connection lines
Grouping of spoke types (producers vs consumers)
Optional data-flow annotations on spokes
Boundary circle or ring around the hub
diagramTypes.style_hint: Hub centered, spoke nodes evenly distributed at equal radius, spokes grouped by type (left = sources, right = consumers), connection lines with flow direction, and connections primarily between hub and spokes rather than spoke-to-spoke unless explicitly requested
diagramTypes.color_palette: Hub in bold brand color (deep blue or purple), spokes in lighter category colors, white background, thin gray connection lines

diagramTypes.name: Node-Edge Graph
diagramTypes.description: A network or knowledge graph showing relationships between entities, microservices, or data objects as nodes connected by labeled edges
key_elements: Circular or rounded-rectangle nodes (one per entity)
Labeled directed or undirected edges between nodes
Node clusters / communities highlighted by background shading
Edge weight or label indicating relationship type
Legend for node types and edge types
Optional force-directed organic layout
diagramTypes.style_hint: Organic or force-directed layout, node size proportional to importance/degree, cluster grouping with soft background zones, clear edge labels, and only the stated relationships drawn so the graph does not invent extra edges or fully connect clusters
diagramTypes.color_palette: Distinct color per node cluster (blue, green, orange, red), white node labels, semi-transparent edge lines, white or dark background

diagramTypes.name: Decision Tree / Flowchart
diagramTypes.description: A logical branching flow showing 'If X then Y' paths for architectural or process choices, guiding users to a recommendation or outcome; each decision must branch to distinct next nodes or terminal outcomes instead of being flattened into one serial chain
use_cases: Architecture selection guide (e.g., 'Do you need real-time? → Kafka vs Batch')
key_elements: Start oval at the top
Diamond decision nodes with Yes/No (or labeled) branches that attach to the correct outgoing edge
Rectangular process/action boxes
Terminal outcome boxes at the leaves (highlighted), with one outcome per branch leaf unless the topic explicitly merges paths
Clear directional arrows on all paths
Optional color-coding by path outcome type
diagramTypes.style_hint: Top-to-bottom or left-to-right layout, decision diamonds clearly distinct from action rectangles, outcome boxes at edges in a distinct color, no crossing arrows, and explicit branch structure where alternatives split from each decision rather than continuing as one central vertical trunk
diagramTypes.color_palette: Blue start, yellow diamonds for decisions, white rectangles for actions, green terminal boxes for positive outcomes, red for warnings, white background

diagramTypes.name: Before & After Sequence
diagramTypes.description: A two-panel diagram contrasting a legacy/problematic architecture ('Before') with a modern/improved approach ('After'), highlighting key differences
key_elements: Two clearly labeled panels: 'Before' (left/top) and 'After' (right/bottom)
Same system scope shown in both panels for direct comparison
Problem indicators in 'Before' (red warnings, tangled arrows, bottlenecks)
Improvement indicators in 'After' (green highlights, clean flows, added components)
Central divider with transformation label or arrow
Callout annotations pointing to key changes
diagramTypes.style_hint: Side-by-side layout, 'Before' panel in muted/warm tones (red/orange accents for problems), 'After' panel in cooler clean tones (green/blue for improvements), bold section headers, and strict separation between panels so elements do not blend into one mixed architecture
diagramTypes.color_palette: Before: warm gray background, red/orange problem highlights. After: light blue/white background, green improvement highlights. Dark callout labels

diagramTypes.name: Logo Generator
diagramTypes.description: A professional logo design for a company, project, product, or brand — including mark, wordmark, and color palette
key_elements: Primary logomark or icon (symbol representing the brand)
Wordmark / logotype with chosen typeface
Horizontal and stacked lockup variants
Brand color palette with primary and accent colors
Monochrome / single-color version
Short brand rationale or concept note
diagramTypes.style_hint: Clean, scalable vector aesthetic — minimal details that read well at any size, strong silhouette, balanced negative space, and a single memorable shape or motif
diagramTypes.color_palette: Defined by the brand concept: bold accent on white or dark background, with one or two supporting neutrals

diagramTypes.name: Icon Set Grid
diagramTypes.description: A structured grid of related icons designed as a cohesive family, ideal for product systems, pitch decks, and design handoff slides
key_elements: Uniform grid layout with 6 to 16 icons
Consistent stroke, fill, and corner radius treatment
Short labels beneath each icon
Optional category grouping or color coding
One highlighted hero icon or key feature
Background guides or sizing keylines
diagramTypes.style_hint: Symmetric grid with consistent spacing, shared visual grammar across all icons, optional caption row, and one or two accent colors for emphasis
diagramTypes.color_palette: White or pale gray background, dark neutral strokes, one or two accent colors such as blue and coral

diagramTypes.name: Title Slide Hero Layout
diagramTypes.description: A keynote-style cover slide combining a bold title area with a large hero diagram or illustration for presentation openings and section dividers
key_elements: Large presentation title block
Subtitle or short supporting statement
Hero visual occupying one half or two thirds of the slide
Optional small logo or presenter name area
Background shape system or gradient field
Clear visual hierarchy optimized for distant viewing
diagramTypes.style_hint: 16:9 slide composition with strong title hierarchy, large margin structure, asymmetric hero placement, and minimal supporting text
diagramTypes.color_palette: Bold presentation gradient or solid brand background, high-contrast title text, bright accent highlights

diagramTypes.name: Chevron Process Slide
diagramTypes.description: A slide-friendly horizontal process diagram using large chevrons or segmented arrows to explain phased workflows, roadmaps, or operating models
key_elements: 3 to 7 large chevron stages
Step titles and short sublabels
Optional icons inside each stage
Progressive directional flow from left to right
Milestone markers or ownership tags
Headline and footer note zone for slide context
diagramTypes.style_hint: Wide 16:9 layout with oversized chevrons, concise stage labels, strong contrast between phases, and minimal clutter for readability on slides, keeping each phase in its own chevron rather than breaking the process into scattered boxes
diagramTypes.color_palette: Sequential brand colors across stages, white or dark neutral background, high-contrast text

diagramTypes.name: KPI Dashboard Slide
diagramTypes.description: A presentation-ready dashboard summarizing metrics, status indicators, and small charts in a single polished overview slide
key_elements: Large KPI cards for headline metrics
Mini charts or sparklines
Status badges or trend arrows
Segmented sections for categories or teams
Optional commentary callouts
Consistent card alignment across a slide grid
diagramTypes.style_hint: Presentation dashboard arranged on a clean modular grid, prominent top-row KPIs, small supporting visuals below, and restrained annotation, with cards aligned to the grid and metrics kept inside their own panels rather than forming a flowchart
diagramTypes.color_palette: White or dark slate dashboard canvas, blue and green for positive metrics, amber and red for risks, neutral card borders

visualStyles.name: Clean Tech / Flat Design
visualStyles.description: Flat icons, bold color-coded sections, crisp edges, white background, sans-serif labels. Minimal shadows. Best for documentation and technical audiences.

visualStyles.name: Isometric 3D
visualStyles.description: 3D perspective with depth, shadows, and gradient fills. Isometric grid alignment. High visual impact for presentations and executive decks.

visualStyles.name: Blueprint / Engineering
visualStyles.description: Dark navy background, white or cyan technical lines, engineering/schematic aesthetic. Monochromatic with accent highlights. Great for infrastructure and network diagrams.

visualStyles.name: Infographic / Presentation
visualStyles.description: Colorful sections, large illustrative icons, banner-style labels, presentation-ready layout. Best for non-technical stakeholders and slide decks.

visualStyles.name: Hand-Drawn / Whiteboard
visualStyles.description: Rough sketch aesthetic, pencil-style or marker lines, sticky-note colors, informal and brainstorming feel. Ideal for workshops and early-stage ideation.

visualStyles.name: Neon Dark / Cyberpunk
visualStyles.description: Dark background, glowing neon-colored elements, bright edge lines. Cyberpunk or data-mesh aesthetic. High contrast, suitable for tech conferences and digital media.

visualStyles.name: Minimal Outline / Editorial
visualStyles.description: Black-and-white or two-tone, thin outlines only, clean editorial or documentation style. No fills, maximum readability in print or monochrome contexts.

visualStyles.name: Split Screen / Before & After
visualStyles.description: Full-bleed two-panel layout with strong visual contrast between panels. Left side in muted/dark/messy style, right side in clean/bright/modern style.

visualStyles.name: Circular Gradient / Flow Loop
visualStyles.description: Smooth color gradient along a circular or elliptical path, pill-shaped stage labels. Ideal for conveying continuous, iterative processes.

visualStyles.name: Radar / Spider Chart
visualStyles.description: Overlapping polygon radar chart showing multi-dimensional comparison across criteria axes. Best for comparing 3+ options across 4+ dimensions simultaneously.

visualStyles.name: Conference Room Whiteboard
visualStyles.description: Diagram drawn on a large office whiteboard inside a modern meeting room, with realistic dry-erase marker strokes, subtle reflections, pinned notes, and natural presentation context.

visualStyles.name: Glass Board Workshop
visualStyles.description: Semi-transparent glass wall board style with layered marker lines, sticky notes, workshop annotations, and a creative studio atmosphere. Slight parallax and hand-drawn energy.

visualStyles.name: macOS Window Showcase
visualStyles.description: Diagram presented inside a polished macOS application window with traffic-light controls, soft shadows, rounded corners, sidebar or toolbar chrome, and clean Apple-inspired UI framing.

visualStyles.name: Control Panel / Console
visualStyles.description: Futuristic panel or operations console aesthetic with segmented modules, indicator lights, tactical lines, dark surfaces, and precision UI framing around the diagram.

visualStyles.name: MacBook Desk Mockup
visualStyles.description: Diagram displayed on a MacBook screen in a premium desk setup, with device bezel, keyboard edge, subtle perspective, ambient lighting, and a refined product mockup presentation feel.

visualStyles.name: Brand Style Guide
visualStyles.description: Mood-board layout with color swatches, typeface specimens, logo lockup previews, and usage examples arranged on a clean editorial canvas. Ideal for branding presentations and design system documentation.

visualStyles.name: Notebook / Sketchpad
visualStyles.description: Lined or dotted notebook paper texture with pencil or ink sketch aesthetic, hand-lettered labels, and margin annotations. Ideal for concept ideation, logo exploration, and early-stage diagram drafts.

colorPresets.name: Light
colorPresets.description: White or very light gray background with dark text and colored accents. Maximum readability for documentation and presentations.

colorPresets.name: Dark
colorPresets.description: Deep gray or near-black background with light-colored elements and bright accents. High contrast for digital-first or dark-mode contexts.

colorPresets.name: Neutral
colorPresets.description: Muted mid-tone palette with soft grays, beige, and slate. Understated and professional for formal reports and internal documentation.

colorPresets.name: Vibrant
colorPresets.description: Saturated, high-energy color palette with bold fills and bright contrasts. Ideal for presentations targeting non-technical or executive audiences.

colorPresets.name: Monochrome
colorPresets.description: Single hue or pure black-and-white palette. Clean and print-friendly, best for minimal, editorial, or high-contrast accessibility contexts.

colorPresets.name: Brand Blue
colorPresets.description: Corporate blue-dominant palette with white and gray supporting tones. Conveys trust, technology, and professionalism.

colorPresets.name: Warm
colorPresets.description: Warm orange, amber, and soft red tones with light neutral backgrounds. Energetic and approachable for workshop and team-facing materials.

colorPresets.name: Cool
colorPresets.description: Cool cyan, teal, and lavender tones with white or dark backgrounds. Calm, modern feel suited for technical and data-heavy diagrams.

json.promptTemplate:
# Diagram Image Generator — Prompt Template

You are an expert technical illustrator and diagram designer. Your task is to generate a short, precise **image generation prompt** for creating a professional diagram.

## Instructions

Based on the following inputs, craft a single, self-contained image generation prompt optimized for DALL-E 3 or a similar image model. The output must be ONLY the image prompt — no explanations, no markdown, no preamble.

## Inputs

- **Diagram Type:** {{DIAGRAM_TYPE}}
- **Diagram Description (layout rules, key elements, structural guidance):** {{DIAGRAM_TYPE_DESCRIPTION}}
- **Topic / Content:** {{DIAGRAM_TOPIC}}
- **Visual Style:** {{VISUAL_STYLE}}
- **Visual Style Description (rendering rules, color guidance):** {{VISUAL_STYLE_DESCRIPTION}}
- **Color Preset:** {{COLOR_PRESET}}
- **Additional Context:** {{DIAGRAM_CONTEXT}}

## How to use the inputs

1. Use **Diagram Type** as the base diagram label.
2. Apply **Diagram Description** to define only the essential structure and layout.
3. Use **Topic / Content** to add only the most important labels, components, and relationships.
4. Apply **Visual Style** and **Visual Style Description** briefly.
5. Use **Color Preset** as the main palette instruction.
6. Incorporate **Additional Context** only if it improves clarity.
7. If the inputs mention optional notes, side labels, edge labels, callouts, or outcome boxes, attach each one only to the specific node, edge, or branch it belongs to. If the mapping is unclear, omit it rather than inventing or implying that every item gets the same annotation.

## Output Requirements

The image prompt you generate must:

1. Be concise and brief.
2. Stay within 1 short paragraph or 4-5 short sentences.
3. Limit the diagram to at most 10 primary items, nodes, boxes, or steps unless the input clearly requires fewer or more.
4. Avoid long enumerations, extra decoration, or too many labels.
5. If the diagram is a process, workflow, or dependency graph, describe the structure with DAG notation. Use linear notation only for straight sequences such as `A -> B -> C`. For branching flows, write explicit branches such as `Start -> Decision`, `Decision(Yes) -> Outcome A`, `Decision(No) -> Outcome B`, and keep the branches acyclic.
6. Mention structure, style, and palette in minimal wording.
7. End with a short rendering cue only.
8. All texts that must be included in the prompt should be double-quoted, like "SOME TEXT".
9. Never use generic wording such as "add labels X and Y" unless those labels apply to all primary items. When labels or notes apply only to certain items, explicitly bind each one to its target, for example: "attach note \"High maintenance\" to \"Argo Workflows\"".
10. Do not infer symmetric annotations. If only some branches, nodes, or edges have notes, state only those specific attachments.
11. For decision trees and flowcharts, do not collapse multiple decision outcomes into one vertical chain. Each decision branch must point directly to its own next node or terminal outcome.
12. Use quoted edge labels such as "Yes" and "No" only when they are meaningful to distinguish branches, and attach them to the correct outgoing edge.

## Output Format

```text
"[DIAGRAM_TYPE] diagram of [TOPIC_SUMMARY]. Show up to 4-6 key items only. [Short structural/layout instruction]. [If flow matters, use DAG notation: linear flows as A -> B -> C; branching flows as Start -> Decision, Decision(Yes) -> Outcome A, Decision(No) -> Outcome B]. [Only if explicitly mapped, attach notes/callouts to their specific nodes or edges]. Style: [VISUAL_STYLE]. Palette: [COLOR_PRESET]. Clean vector diagram, crisp labels."
```

Now generate the image prompt for the inputs above.
json.diagramDescription:

As a home laboratory its main personal benefits are:

- **Hands-On Experience**
- **Experimentation and Innovation**
- **Problem-Solving Skills**
- **Portfolio Development**
- **Self-Directed Learning**
- **Understanding of Hardware and Networking**
- **Passion and Motivation**

## Goals

- Deploy and maintain a general purpose architecture based on Kubernetes.
- Implement the lastest Devops pratices to automate the building, testing, and deployment of software.
- Incorporate monitoring and security tools to track the performance of software and identify potential issues of any kind.
- Desing and implement a scalable system that allows for the efficient collection, storage, processing, and analysis of Data.

# Diagram Image Generator — Prompt Template

You are an expert technical illustrator and diagram designer. Your task is to generate a short, precise **image generation prompt** for creating a professional diagram.

## Instructions

Based on the following inputs, craft a single, self-contained image generation prompt optimized for DALL-E 3 or a similar image model. The output must be ONLY the image prompt — no explanations, no markdown, no preamble.

## Inputs

- **Diagram Type:** {{DIAGRAM_TYPE}}
- **Diagram Description (layout rules, key elements, structural guidance):** {{DIAGRAM_TYPE_DESCRIPTION}}
- **Topic / Content:** {{DIAGRAM_TOPIC}}
- **Visual Style:** {{VISUAL_STYLE}}
- **Visual Style Description (rendering rules, color guidance):** {{VISUAL_STYLE_DESCRIPTION}}
- **Color Preset:** {{COLOR_PRESET}}
- **Additional Context:** {{DIAGRAM_CONTEXT}}

## How to use the inputs

1. Use **Diagram Type** as the base diagram label.
2. Apply **Diagram Description** to define only the essential structure and layout.
3. Use **Topic / Content** to add only the most important labels, components, and relationships.
4. Apply **Visual Style** and **Visual Style Description** briefly.
5. Use **Color Preset** as the main palette instruction.
6. Incorporate **Additional Context** only if it improves clarity.
7. If the inputs mention optional notes, side labels, edge labels, callouts, or outcome boxes, attach each one only to the specific node, edge, or branch it belongs to. If the mapping is unclear, omit it rather than inventing or implying that every item gets the same annotation.

## Output Requirements

The image prompt you generate must:

1. Be concise and brief.
2. Stay within 1 short paragraph or 4-5 short sentences.
3. Limit the diagram to at most 10 primary items, nodes, boxes, or steps unless the input clearly requires fewer or more.
4. Avoid long enumerations, extra decoration, or too many labels.
5. If the diagram is a process, workflow, or dependency graph, describe the structure with DAG notation. Use linear notation only for straight sequences such as `A -> B -> C`. For branching flows, write explicit branches such as `Start -> Decision`, `Decision(Yes) -> Outcome A`, `Decision(No) -> Outcome B`, and keep the branches acyclic.
6. Mention structure, style, and palette in minimal wording.
7. End with a short rendering cue only.
8. All texts that must be included in the prompt should be double-quoted, like "SOME TEXT".
9. Never use generic wording such as "add labels X and Y" unless those labels apply to all primary items. When labels or notes apply only to certain items, explicitly bind each one to its target, for example: "attach note \"High maintenance\" to \"Argo Workflows\"".
10. Do not infer symmetric annotations. If only some branches, nodes, or edges have notes, state only those specific attachments.
11. For decision trees and flowcharts, do not collapse multiple decision outcomes into one vertical chain. Each decision branch must point directly to its own next node or terminal outcome.
12. Use quoted edge labels such as "Yes" and "No" only when they are meaningful to distinguish branches, and attach them to the correct outgoing edge.

## Output Format

```text
"[DIAGRAM_TYPE] diagram of [TOPIC_SUMMARY]. Show up to 4-6 key items only. [Short structural/layout instruction]. [If flow matters, use DAG notation: linear flows as A -> B -> C; branching flows as Start -> Decision, Decision(Yes) -> Outcome A, Decision(No) -> Outcome B]. [Only if explicitly mapped, attach notes/callouts to their specific nodes or edges]. Style: [VISUAL_STYLE]. Palette: [COLOR_PRESET]. Clean vector diagram, crisp labels."
```

Now generate the image prompt for the inputs above.

visualStyles.name: Clean Tech / Flat Design
visualStyles.description: Flat icons, bold color-coded sections, crisp edges, white background, sans-serif labels. Minimal shadows. Best for documentation and technical audiences.

visualStyles.name: Isometric 3D
visualStyles.description: 3D perspective with depth, shadows, and gradient fills. Isometric grid alignment. High visual impact for presentations and executive decks.

visualStyles.name: Neon Dark / Cyberpunk
visualStyles.description: Dark background, glowing neon-colored elements, bright edge lines. Cyberpunk or data-mesh aesthetic. High contrast, suitable for tech conferences and digital media.

visualStyles.name: Minimal Outline / Editorial
visualStyles.description: Black-and-white or two-tone, thin outlines only, clean editorial or documentation style. No fills, maximum readability in print or monochrome contexts.

visualStyles.name: MacBook Desk Mockup
visualStyles.description: Diagram displayed on a MacBook screen in a premium desk setup, with device bezel, keyboard edge, subtle perspective, ambient lighting, and a refined product mockup presentation feel.

visualStyles.name: Brand Style Guide
visualStyles.description: Mood-board layout with color swatches, typeface specimens, logo lockup previews, and usage examples arranged on a clean editorial canvas. Ideal for branding presentations and design system documentation.

visualStyles.name: Notebook / Sketchpad
visualStyles.description: Lined or dotted notebook paper texture with pencil or ink sketch aesthetic, hand-lettered labels, and margin annotations. Ideal for concept ideation, logo exploration, and early-stage diagram drafts.

colorPresets.name: Light
colorPresets.description: White or very light gray background with dark text and colored accents. Maximum readability for documentation and presentations.

colorPresets.name: Dark
colorPresets.description: Deep gray or near-black background with light-colored elements and bright accents. High contrast for digital-first or dark-mode contexts.

colorPresets.name: Neutral
colorPresets.description: Muted mid-tone palette with soft grays, beige, and slate. Understated and professional for formal reports and internal documentation.

colorPresets.name: Vibrant
colorPresets.description: Saturated, high-energy color palette with bold fills and bright contrasts. Ideal for presentations targeting non-technical or executive audiences.

colorPresets.name: Monochrome
colorPresets.description: Single hue or pure black-and-white palette. Clean and print-friendly, best for minimal, editorial, or high-contrast accessibility contexts.

colorPresets.name: Brand Blue
colorPresets.description: Corporate blue-dominant palette with white and gray supporting tones. Conveys trust, technology, and professionalism.

colorPresets.name: Warm
colorPresets.description: Warm orange, amber, and soft red tones with light neutral backgrounds. Energetic and approachable for workshop and team-facing materials.

colorPresets.name: Cool
colorPresets.description: Cool cyan, teal, and lavender tones with white or dark backgrounds. Calm, modern feel suited for technical and data-heavy diagrams.

json.Image Prompt: "Logo design of a minimalist, tech-focused icon for 'DataHub.local'. The central mark features a stylized, glowing geometric node connected to a hexagonal mesh pattern, symbolizing a lightweight data platform. The wordmark 'DataHub.local' is written in a clean, modern sans-serif font beneath the icon. Style: Neon Dark Cyberpunk with a deep gray background (oklch 0.28 0.04 260.05). Palette: Electric cyan, magenta, and white glowing edge lines. High-contrast vector graphic, scalable, professional digital-first aesthetic."

json.diagramDescription:
DataHub.local is a home laboratory that will enable you to create an complete data platform like [Snowflake](https://www.snowflake.com/) but that could run in low-end devices such as RaspberryPi, or OrangePi at home.

As a home laboratory its main personal benefits are:

- **Hands-On Experience**
- **Experimentation and Innovation**
- **Problem-Solving Skills**
- **Portfolio Development**
- **Self-Directed Learning**
- **Understanding of Hardware and Networking**
- **Passion and Motivation**

## Goals

- Deploy and maintain a general purpose architecture based on Kubernetes.
- Implement the lastest Devops pratices to automate the building, testing, and deployment of software.
- Incorporate monitoring and security tools to track the performance of software and identify potential issues of any kind.
- Desing and implement a scalable system that allows for the efficient collection, storage, processing, and analysis of Data.
json.generatedSystemPrompt:
# Diagram Image Generator — Prompt Template

You are an expert technical illustrator and diagram designer. Your task is to generate a short, precise **image generation prompt** for creating a professional diagram.

## Instructions

Based on the following inputs, craft a single, self-contained image generation prompt optimized for DALL-E 3 or a similar image model. The output must be ONLY the image prompt — no explanations, no markdown, no preamble.

## Inputs

- **Diagram Type:** Logo Generator
- **Diagram Description (layout rules, key elements, structural guidance):** A professional logo design for a company, project, product, or brand — including mark, wordmark, and color palette Clean, scalable vector aesthetic — minimal details that read well at any size, strong silhouette, balanced negative space, and a single memorable shape or motif
- **Topic / Content:** DataHub.local is a home laboratory that will enable you to create an complete data platform like [Snowflake](https://www.snowflake.com/) but that could run in low-end devices such as RaspberryPi, or OrangePi at home.

As a home laboratory its main personal benefits are:

- **Hands-On Experience**
- **Experimentation and Innovation**
- **Problem-Solving Skills**
- **Portfolio Development**
- **Self-Directed Learning**
- **Understanding of Hardware and Networking**
- **Passion and Motivation**

## Goals

- Deploy and maintain a general purpose architecture based on Kubernetes.
- Implement the lastest Devops pratices to automate the building, testing, and deployment of software.
- Incorporate monitoring and security tools to track the performance of software and identify potential issues of any kind.
- Desing and implement a scalable system that allows for the efficient collection, storage, processing, and analysis of Data.
- **Visual Style:** Neon Dark / Cyberpunk
- **Visual Style Description (rendering rules, color guidance):** Dark background, glowing neon-colored elements, bright edge lines. Cyberpunk or data-mesh aesthetic. High contrast, suitable for tech conferences and digital media.
- **Color Preset:** Dark: Deep gray or near-black background with light-colored elements and bright accents. High contrast for digital-first or dark-mode contexts.
- **Additional Context:** Use background color "oklch(0.28 0.04 260.05)"

## How to use the inputs

1. Use **Diagram Type** as the base diagram label.
2. Apply **Diagram Description** to define only the essential structure and layout.
3. Use **Topic / Content** to add only the most important labels, components, and relationships.
4. Apply **Visual Style** and **Visual Style Description** briefly.
5. Use **Color Preset** as the main palette instruction.
6. Incorporate **Additional Context** only if it improves clarity.
7. If the inputs mention optional notes, side labels, edge labels, callouts, or outcome boxes, attach each one only to the specific node, edge, or branch it belongs to. If the mapping is unclear, omit it rather than inventing or implying that every item gets the same annotation.

## Output Requirements

The image prompt you generate must:

1. Be concise and brief.
2. Stay within 1 short paragraph or 4-5 short sentences.
3. Limit the diagram to at most 10 primary items, nodes, boxes, or steps unless the input clearly requires fewer or more.
4. Avoid long enumerations, extra decoration, or too many labels.
5. If the diagram is a process, workflow, or dependency graph, describe the structure with DAG notation. Use linear notation only for straight sequences such as `A -> B -> C`. For branching flows, write explicit branches such as `Start -> Decision`, `Decision(Yes) -> Outcome A`, `Decision(No) -> Outcome B`, and keep the branches acyclic.
6. Mention structure, style, and palette in minimal wording.
7. End with a short rendering cue only.
8. All texts that must be included in the prompt should be double-quoted, like "SOME TEXT".
9. Never use generic wording such as "add labels X and Y" unless those labels apply to all primary items. When labels or notes apply only to certain items, explicitly bind each one to its target, for example: "attach note \"High maintenance\" to \"Argo Workflows\"".
10. Do not infer symmetric annotations. If only some branches, nodes, or edges have notes, state only those specific attachments.
11. For decision trees and flowcharts, do not collapse multiple decision outcomes into one vertical chain. Each decision branch must point directly to its own next node or terminal outcome.
12. Use quoted edge labels such as "Yes" and "No" only when they are meaningful to distinguish branches, and attach them to the correct outgoing edge.

## Output Format

```text
"[DIAGRAM_TYPE] diagram of [TOPIC_SUMMARY]. Show up to 4-6 key items only. [Short structural/layout instruction]. [If flow matters, use DAG notation: linear flows as A -> B -> C; branching flows as Start -> Decision, Decision(Yes) -> Outcome A, Decision(No) -> Outcome B]. [Only if explicitly mapped, attach notes/callouts to their specific nodes or edges]. Style: [VISUAL_STYLE]. Palette: [COLOR_PRESET]. Clean vector diagram, crisp labels."
```

Now generate the image prompt for the inputs above.
json.suggestedImagePrompt:
"Logo Generator diagram of DataHub.local is a home laboratory that will enable you to create an complete data platform like [Snowflake](https://www.snowflake.com/) but that could run in low-end devices such as RaspberryPi, or OrangePi at home.

As a home laboratory its main personal benefits are:

- **Hands-On Experience**
- **Experimentation and Innovation**
- **Problem-Solving Skills**
- **Portfolio Development**
- **Self-Directed Learning**
- **Understanding of Hardware and Networking**
- **Passion and Motivation**

## Goals

- Deploy and maintain a general purpose architecture based on Kubernetes.
- Implement the lastest Devops pratices to automate the building, testing, and deployment of software.
- Incorporate monitoring and security tools to track the performance of software and identify potential issues of any kind.
- Desing and implement a scalable system that allows for the efficient collection, storage, processing, and analysis of Data.. Show at most 4-6 key elements. If flow matters, use A -> B -> C. Style: Neon Dark / Cyberpunk. Palette: Dark. Clean vector lines, short labels."
